text: Add src/client/ApiEaseCreateRequestClient.js as the canonical Node client for calling the APIEase Create Request API.
goal:
Develop the initial apiease-cli project that provides a Node core client capable of creating requests through the APIEase Create Request API contract, along with a thin bash wrapper that allows users and automation tools to invoke the client from the command line.

Goal

Create a new open source project named apiease-cli that implements a Node based core client for interacting with the APIEase Create Request API. The Node core client will be the canonical client implementation used by command line scripts, automation tools, and AI agents. The CLI layer will act only as a thin wrapper that invokes the Node core client.

The purpose of this task is to establish the client side contract that matches the API implemented in the apiease project and to demonstrate an end to end flow where a request definition can be submitted locally and created remotely through the API.

Scope

Implement only the minimal functionality required to support creating requests through the APIEase API. The task should establish the structure of the apiease-cli project, implement the Node core client capable of calling the Create Request API endpoint, and provide a bash script that invokes the Node client.

The CLI should support creating requests from a structured input file and sending the request configuration to the APIEase backend through the public API.

Requirements

Create a new project named apiease-cli intended to be published as an open source repository. The project must include a Node core client that acts as the primary implementation layer for interacting with the APIEase API.

The Node core client must implement a function responsible for creating a request through the APIEase API. This function must accept a structured object representing the request configuration and must send that object to the Create Request API endpoint implemented in the apiease project.

The Node core client must support authentication using the APIEase API key so that CLI users and automation tools can interact with the API.

The Node core client must perform lightweight contract validation before making the API call. Validation should confirm that the provided payload is a valid structured object and matches the expected request contract shape, but it should not attempt to duplicate full backend validation logic.

The Node core client must return structured JSON responses that clearly indicate success or failure and must surface backend validation errors in a machine readable format.

Create a thin bash wrapper script that invokes the Node core client. The bash wrapper must accept arguments indicating that a request should be created and must pass the structured request configuration to the Node client. The wrapper should not implement business logic and should only act as a bridge between the command line and the Node client.

The CLI command must support reading the request configuration from a JSON file so that requests can be authored locally and submitted to the API.

The CLI must produce output that is readable for humans but must also support machine friendly output so that automation tools and AI agents can reliably interpret the result.

Implementation guidance

The Node core client should be the canonical implementation layer and should encapsulate all logic related to interacting with the APIEase API. The bash script must remain minimal and should simply forward input arguments or file input to the Node client.

The Node core client must be designed so that it can be called both by the CLI wrapper and directly by other Node based tools. The architecture should keep the client logic independent from the CLI wrapper so that the same client can be reused by other automation layers.

The request creation contract used by the Node core client must match the API contract implemented in the apiease project. The CLI must not introduce its own schema independent of the API.

Avoid implementing additional commands such as pull, push, variables, widgets, diff, status, or synchronization features. This task must focus only on the minimal vertical slice needed to create a request through the API.

Deliverable

A working apiease-cli project containing a Node core client capable of creating requests through the APIEase API and a bash wrapper command that allows users and automation tools to submit request configurations from the command line. The client must authenticate using the APIEase API key, validate the input structure, call the Create Request API endpoint, and return structured results.
context: Reference the public route behavior in ../apiease/app/routes/api/programmatic.requests.jsx.
Reference the exposed route path in ../apiease/vite.config.js, which maps the API to /api/programmatic/requests.
The API uses x-apiease-api-key and x-shop-myshopify-domain headers.
steps: Expose a public createRequest method that accepts the structured request payload plus the connection details needed to call APIEase.
Run the lightweight contract validator before issuing the HTTP request.
POST the payload as application/json to /api/programmatic/requests with the required authentication and shop headers.
Parse success and failure responses into a consistent machine-readable result shape for direct Node consumers.
constraints: Keep all API interaction logic in this Node client rather than in the CLI layer.
Do not print to stdout or stderr from the core client.
Do not mutate the request into a new CLI-specific contract before sending it.
acceptanceCriteria: The client can be called directly by other Node-based tools and AI agents through a createRequest method.
Successful calls preserve the backend success payload shape of ok, shopDomain, and request, while also surfacing the HTTP status for automation.
Backend failures preserve machine-readable errorCode, message, fieldErrors, and status information.
Local contract validation failures short-circuit without making a network request.
